title: Matt and Jerusalem read “Silent Spring”
subtitle: Can a return to the classic text save environmentalism?
contentHtml:
Paid episode

Matt and Jerusalem read “Silent Spring”

I am not a huge fan of America’s major environmental institutions.

I believe that pollution is bad and climate change is real, and I think it’s important that policymakers take that into account. But the worldview, ideology, and priorities of the green establishment strike me as significantly askew from the imperatives of economic growth and democratic accountability. So when I saw that Rachel Carson’s 1962 book, Silent Spring, is now marketed on the cover as “the classic that launched the environmental movement,” I expected to find a book that I hated.

But as is often the case, the classics are classic for a reason. Carson’s specific arguments about the over-use of pesticides are very convincing. She has an eco-modernist streak in which she endorses the sterilization techniques that were successfully used to fight the New World screwworm as an alternative to spraying. Importantly, she was just factually correct to raise the point that insects’ very rapid life cycles mean that they tend to quickly evolve resistance to insecticides.

Jerusalem in particular really got pretty Carson-pilled, seeing her as someone who built her case patiently, marshaled evidence, and won.

I’m a little more cynical. Despite the book having real strengths, you can also see the fatal flaws of green ideology all the way through the text. Carson repeatedly expresses a very backward-looking romantic nostalgia. She doesn’t really distinguish between actual wilderness and just old-timey farms that she thinks are picturesque. She doesn’t seem interested in economic growth or human poverty. And Jerusalem and I agree that Carson is unduly skeptical of the normal political process and the idea of just persuading elected officials to do different stuff if you want them to weigh the balance of considerations around conservation issues differently. Modern environmentalism has instead embraced the idea that true democracy somehow involves endless rounds of consultation and post hoc litigation, and while I wish you could say that’s untrue to the core text, I’m not really convinced that it is.

Has the glass been half full or half empty all along? Does the green movement need to get back to its roots or does an informed approach to environmental issues need to cut loose from those roots?

We get into all of it on this week’s episode of The Argument.

Watch or listen wherever you get your podcasts.

The transcript will be available below the paywall in this post for paying subscribers.

WATCH THE EPISODE HERE

New episodes post every Thursday. For an ad-free version and the full transcript, subscribe at TheArgumentMag.com.

Jerusalem Demsas: Hi, I’m Jerusalem Demsas.

Matthew Yglesias: I’m Matthew Yglesias.

Jerusalem Demsas: Welcome to The Argument. This week, Matt and I read Silent Spring, a book that launched the modern environmental movement. And I liked it way more than I thought I was going to.

Matthew Yglesias: Because you hate nature?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: Animals? Trees?

Jerusalem Demsas: I absolutely hate nature. I mean, in general — as someone who’s written a lot about housing and the need to build more housing, and I care a lot about transit, I’m an urbanist — I’ve been very frustrated by a lot of parts of the modern environmental movement that have been anti-modernity, anti-science in some respects. And I was expecting a lot more of that from Rachel Carson’s book, because of the movement she spawned, but…

Matthew Yglesias: Well, I gotta say, I mean, you flip this book open — or, in my case, read it digitally — the very first page, though, is like, “Picture a cutesy old-time farm, and the chickens are clucking, and, like, soon it’s doom,” you know?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, it is a little insane.

Matthew Yglesias: And it has this very, you know, like the industrial era of Mordor is despoiling the Shire. And I was like, “All right, this is exactly what aggravates me about these people.” And it’s in there.

But most of the book is actually pretty boring. It’s a lot of very — I don’t want to say tedious — it’s a lot of very specific information about specific instances in which insecticide was sprayed in some community, and then there were some negative consequences of spraying the insecticide. There’s a little bit of herbicide spraying, a lot of insecticide spraying, and, given its reputation, it’s just kind of narrower than I thought it was going to be.

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, the book sets in, as Matt says, opens up with this sort of fable about an idyllic town, and that chapter ends — and this is also the way her famous New Yorker piece also starts off, which is what most people probably read. I don’t think people really read this book, because of how dense it is.

But the entire book is really like a scientist’s piece-by-piece case study of what she is witnessing. And she’s a scientist, so that makes sense. But the way people talk about Silent Spring now — I mean, even, again, this cover, “The classic that launched the modern environmental movement” — I really expected it to be this diatribe against modernity and these really far-out-there claims, but, in reality, it just seems largely correct: a bunch of statements that follow each other that are all difficult to really grapple with.

Matthew Yglesias: So, I guess to pull this out: The story she tells, which, I mean, I think is historically correct, is that there was a lot of development in the chemical industry associated with the World Wars. And then, after the wars, there was a push to find civilian uses for these kinds of chemicals.

nd one of the main things that they discovered was that certain neurotoxic agents that were related to things that were used in chemical warfare in World War I could be used in attenuated forms, or lower doses, to kill insects. Because insects are small compared to mammals. They, like, absorb material from the environment through their skin, things like that. So you can spray crops with these various different things. DDT is sort of the headline chemical, and you can kill a lot of bugs this way.

And the point of the book, I guess, is that these small doses of insecticide damage birds, mammals, and humans downstream in ways that are not being accounted for correctly by the industry, the farmers, and government officials.

Jerusalem Demsas: And I think the context here is just that there really wasn’t widespread awareness that there was potential damage, like side effects, of widespread DDT.

Right now I’m kind of like, I went into this book sort of like, “Well, yeah, of course, duh, there might be some knock-on effects of spraying shit into the environment.” But my awareness of that is largely downstream of work by Carson to point out that this was the case. And there was a lot of denialism, both from the chemical industry but also regulators, that there was any potential risk whatsoever to wildlife and humans, etc.

I think one thing — because we’re being pretty complimentary at this point — that I’ll point out is that the book often quickly slides between various types of harm in ways that I find very persuasive, but somewhat deceptive from a more critical point of view.

Sometimes she’s talking about, “Oh, this is hurting birds.” Sometimes she’s like, “Oh, wow, doesn’t it look ugly? Isn’t there an aesthetic loss because now the beautiful trees and the verdant grasses are gone?” And then sometimes it’s like, “Well, also maybe it causes cancer, and also maybe it doesn’t work because the insects are becoming adaptive and they’re now becoming resistant to the spraying that we’re doing.”

And it’s fine to make all of these specific individual claims, but I think at its core, a lot of people are mostly concerned about the impact to human life and well-being. And most of the evidence really isn’t strongest there. The evidence is mostly really strong in the “it’s hurting the ecosystem, this could eventually have knock-on effects to hurt humans, but largely it seems like it hurts some birds.”

The best evidence that it hurts humans seems to be in very specific cases where there are accidents, when workers are using the chemicals improperly, or an accident happens while they’re working.

But Carson herself dies shortly after this book is published, of breast cancer. And I think that that had a really, really big impact itself on people’s understanding of this. Obviously, it’s very difficult to link where someone got cancer from. Was it a result of the chemical environment she was in? We don’t know that’s not true. But that is something that many people do believe now, and I think that link, to “this is really hurting humans,” is both the least well-supported by the evidence, but the most persuasive element of the whole book.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, I mean, that’s what I wanted to say. One thing about this book is that it doesn’t have a lot of really clear policy prescriptions, and she leans at the end a lot on the argument that the spraying literally doesn’t work, that insects are developing resistance.

And so, when I was a young blogger in the mid-aughts, there was a lot of discourse that was being put forward by the Competitive Enterprise Institute and some other conservative organizations. And they wanted to make people understand that Rachel Carson and her stigmatization of DDT was responsible for the deaths of millions of people by malaria in the Third World.

So, I was familiar with that discourse at that time. And it is both true that human deaths due to malaria is, in my opinion, a much bigger problem than eggshell thinning among bald eagles, which was the signature critique of DDT, but, actually, DDT resistance among mosquitoes became a huge problem by 1970. And the main reason large-scale DDT spraying was phased out wasn’t actually to save the bald eagles. It was because it wasn’t working anymore.

And so both, I think there’s both an unfair attack on Carson that was mounted about this, but also that Carson herself is not being very clear about what she’s saying at times.

Jerusalem Demsas: I don’t know if that’s true. I mean, I think that there are two policy-relevant claims that she’s making: One is that there are more targeted mechanisms one could use.

She talks over and over again about how you don’t have to do this — they were literally just spraying large-scale over towns. There’s this one anecdote that she tells, which I didn’t personally look into the veracity of — our fact-checker will let me know if it’s wrong — but where literally there are these low-flying aircraft that are just dropping all across various parts of this town without telling anyone. So people kind of freak out. They’re like, “Why are these planes really low over whatever?”

And so, that sort of thing, it’s not targeted at all. And then she points to evidence that there’s ways to target the spraying on specific crops that you’re trying to accurately — or specific weeds or herbs that you’re trying to kill, or the places where you really don’t want insects because it’s going to hurt agriculture.

The second policy-relevant claim she makes — and I find this one actually really revealing — is that you could introduce other natural elements into the ecosystem. So, for instance, bugs that would eat the insects, or moths that would eat the insects, or whatever.

I find this super interesting, because the larger claim that she has — the ecological claim she’s making — is that there’s some sort of natural equilibrium that a biosystem will eventually reach if you just “let it be the way it’s supposed to be.” I’m doing air quotes for our auditory listeners.

Matthew Yglesias: That’s what I mean, though. I feel like a lot of the book is this kind of motte and bailey, where — I always forget which one is the motte and which one is the bailey.

Jerusalem Demsas: I don’t know either.

Matthew Yglesias: But I think the motte is that the pesticide industry is out of control, that there are health risks associated with pesticides, and the regulators are not taking those risks seriously at all. And she absolutely convinced me of that. We need to think more clearly about what is happening and why, and for what benefit and at what cost.

But then, there’s this bailey, which is that there’s a natural balance in the ecosystem, there’s the old ways, and that these chemical disruptors are toying with it.

It reminds me of the implicit politics of Frankenstein, right? That we’ve messed with forces that are beyond our control, and there’s going to be hell to pay for this. And notably, the cover of the book, if you hold it up — this is not a sick person; it’s a bird, right?

And a question that — you know, if I could resurrect her, or would have for you, or for anybody — is, insofar as there is a balance between producing food for the rapidly growing human population of the second half of the 20th century and birds: shouldn’t we side with the people?

Jerusalem Demsas: I agree that — I mean, this is again why I expected not to like this book, because I sort of assumed that it was going to come much harder on this question and be like, “Obviously I don’t care about humans as much as I care about general wildlife.” She doesn’t — I mean, just to stick to the text — she doesn’t say any of that. There’s implications, and I think that you raising Frankenstein is really interesting, because in the intro — I don’t know, did you read the Linda Lear intro?

Matthew Yglesias: No, I have a different edition.

Jerusalem Demsas: OK, so she writes about her biography, that she had read widely in the English Romantic tradition and had articulated a personal sense of mission — her quote, “vision splendid.” And so, the English Romantic tradition — I’ve gotten weirdly into this recently, reading a bunch of romantic books — but romanticism is a reaction to the Enlightenment movement, and, not to make everything about this, but in many ways, all debates are sort of between romantic sensibilities and Enlightenment sensibilities, if you’re super reductive about it.

And so, what you’re witnessing here is, the post… like, the World War moment was this real veneration of science, of scientific progress, of our ability to — I mean, even the atom bomb is kind of the moment where we’re like, “Oh shit, have we gone too far here?” And she’s obviously coming at the tail end of this, and so much of her book is a romantic backlash to this moment, to modernity in general.

And she does this in various ways. It’s not just this question here where she is really kind of against progress, but throughout the book, she really articulates a frustration with hubris of man’s ability to dominate nature.

That’s how she actually ends the book — she specifically says the quote, “control of nature is a phrase conceived in arrogance, born of the Neanderthal age of biology and philosophy, when it was supposed that nature exists for the convenience of man.”

And, to me, as someone who finds myself more on, obviously, the Enlightenment side of these debates, I find her affect to be wrong and not properly progress-oriented or human-focused or human-centered. But I do think that, in the moment, this was probably a correct counterbalance to a culture of science that had gotten way ahead of its ability to care for the negative externalities of progress.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, what’s interesting — so, she dies in 1964. The book comes out, what, ‘62?

Jerusalem Demsas: ‘61.

Matthew Yglesias: ‘61. And there’s a big…

Jerusalem Demsas: No, ‘62, you’re right.

Matthew Yglesias: ‘62. CBS does a huge hour-long documentary based on this in 1963, at a time when — Big Three — you know, like a third of Americans would actually watch an hour-long documentary.

And she dies. She dies really fast. So we don’t know what she would have said about things that happen later in life. And a question that we can have is, “Do things that we think happen later in time represent a inappropriate reading of these ideas, or are the ideas present within the text all along?”

It does seem to me that… Like, she absolutely convinces me that there is too much insecticide spraying, and that, in particular, that DDT is actually not a good way to kill mosquitoes. And, clearly, in the cost-benefit calculus, if the point of broad DDT spraying is to prevent disease-bearing mosquitoes, but it doesn’t work, then there’s no good reason to do it.

But it seems to me that it’s a correct reading of — you know, we’re not doing book club on The Population Bomb, but that the line between those books is quite clear and quite direct.

I mean, she’s not really going out of her way to say, “I just have a narrow technical critique of these pesticides.” She does keep coming back to the idea of the harmonic balance of nature that is being disturbed and distended here. And it’s the moments where she breaks that frame that are fascinating.

She has this thing about New World screwworms that I found to be out of step with the rest of the book. OK, so screwworms: This is some kind of bug. It lays larvae in wounds on the skin of cattle and livestock, things like this. It’s apparently a really agonizing death, one of the most horrible ways to go, and also was costly to farmers and ranchers.

So, originally, they wanted to spray against the New World screwworm, but they decided, actually, not to do this, because there aren’t that many New World screwworms. It’s just not that thick on the ground. It wasn’t a good way to do it.

So people came up with this other idea, which was: We could irradiate male screwworms raised in captivity, and that will sterilize them. And then you just drop huge quantities of sterilized male screwworms on the area. They will impregnate the female screwworms, and then they will lay dud eggs, and the whole thing will wipe out.

She says they did this in Florida and it worked. They extended it to Georgia and it works. And she says, “Maybe this could be a promising model that we could expand, but it would be really hard because it might be reinfected from Texas.”

Now, in the real world, we know they not only took this, they took it as far west as Texas, they took it down into Mexico, they took it all the way across Central America. And there is — I don’t know if it’s once a year or once a month or something — but there’s a U.S. government-funded effort ongoing in Panama to bomb the world with irradiated male New World screwworms and prevent the parasite from crossing back into Central America.

So, this is an incredible triumph of science and modernity. And if that was the main theme of the book, I would say this is the greatest book I’ve ever read, and every environmentalist needs to go back to the original text and be like, “This is a book about not being idiots and not being slaves to the interest-group politics of the chemistry industry and looking capaciously at what we can do with science and reason to solve these problems.”

But that screwworm thing is in the book, but it’s not what the book is about.

Jerusalem Demsas: I actually find — I had a very different reading than you. I mean, first of all, it’s important to note that Rachel Carson is a scientist. She has a master’s degree, she’s a biologist — and we’ll get into this later, because that’s the lady science. They let the women do biology. They didn’t like them in chemistry or physics or anything like that — she’s a scientist, she works in the federal government, she’s kind of the head of all of the Department of the Interior’s editorial products, etc.

And she’s writing and editing, and she’s finding, she’s coming across all of these findings, that there are these knock-on effects, or there are, in specific places all across the country or across the world, places where DDT or other insecticides or herbicides are causing problems.

And, while I agree there’s — throughout her book, there’s this kind of romantic sensibility that exists there — she does repeatedly go out of her way to say there are techniques that you can use to address this that are not getting rid of DDT.

She never actually says “we should not use insecticides, we should not use herbicides.” And she suggests even bringing different kinds of moths or whatever into the ecosystems, just like you said.

And so, my reading of it is, actually, I do find this a very persuasive way for environmentalists to go back to... I actually think that if environmentalists today, instead of trying to get a lot of really out-there attention by, I don’t know, stopping traffic or spraying paint on priceless artifacts in museums, were instead doing really, really careful analysis of the ways that damage to the environment was creating harm for things that we care about — life, health, aesthetics, whatever it is — I find that really, really persuasive.

And one thing to note is that part of the reason Carson is so influential is because the reaction to her was so unhinged. I think this is very much one of the first Streisand moments of the modern era, where the chemical industry, when they find this book, they’re looking to sue her publisher for libel. They’re going after her. They’re saying all this crazy stuff about her.

The CBS hour-long special they do is kind of like a documentary. They interview her. They also interview this industry scientist — I mean, he’s a real scientist; he’s not just a paid shill, but he’s in a lab coat. He’s, like, obviously, you know, he’s this man, he’s a PhD, etc. She wasn’t a PhD.

And she comes off — I couldn’t find the whole thing online, I watched parts and clips of it — but she’s really, really chill. She’s just like, “Here is the science that I found.”

Matthew Yglesias: Bari Weiss, if you’re out there, put this back on CBS.

Jerusalem Demsas: But, to me, watching it, she just seems so credible immediately. This guy seems a little bit angry. I don’t know if this is true contemporaneously, but the way it’s read from more modern people, who are obviously more biased in Carson’s direction, is that he was this man yelling about how she was going to destroy all human progress. And, to me, if we’re talking about Enlightenment sensibilities, she has way more of an Enlightenment, rational sensibility than he does throughout the entire thing.

Matthew Yglesias: OK, so, environmentalists, in general: Like, what’s your beef? What’s the problem? Cause you sort of reference this — you reference throwing paint and other stuff like that — but not just on a tactical level...

Jerusalem Demsas: So, I came up through environmental, climate-movement stuff. One of my early internships was at the Climate Action Campaign when I was still in college. I was in D.C. for the summer, and I worked on Climate Action Campaign, which was the big table of all the big environmental groups that was trying to help pass the Clean Power Plan, which was Barack Obama’s signature climate regulation to regulate existing point-source pollution on existing coal plants and power plants.

And I, like many people of my generation, were very, very concerned about climate change. I see it as an existential threat for humans. I see it as a concern for health and for ecosystems, but also just for people to be able to live the lives that they want to, and for human progress.

And so, that’s where I come out of. And then, over the course of the past decade and a half, realizing how few groups in the environmental movement are actually just focused on doing cost-benefit analysis of various policies to figure out “How can we actually reduce the amount of carbon dioxide that is being emitted?” and instead are doing kind of really unproductive things, whether they’re focusing on — I mean, you talk about it all the time, straw bans or whatever — but also focusing on non-environmental or non-climate change aspects of environmentalism, which is, to me, very anti-progress.

So, obviously, I come out of housing. And, to me, one of the biggest things you can do to reduce emissions in the Western world, at least, is that you live in more dense areas. So you build a lot more housing in center cities, you build great transportation networks…

And then you find, over and over again, these green groups are the ones who are the problem in actually developing in a quote-unquote “green way.” And I wrote this piece in The Atlantic years back about this kind of tension with environmentalism around, well, real environmentalists would be really pro a lot of the scientific progress, a lot of this kind of modern building. And instead they find themselves on the side of these conservative views of the way that the world should look. And they’re very anti-change and anti-progress.

And so, that’s my beef with modern environmentalism. But I’m still pro “we should take on climate change.” That’s good.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, I guess I never really… Back when I was more progressive, I was more of a labor guy. And I’m older than you, so this always used to be a fight.

Like, the big factional fight inside the Democratic Party used to be, you know, there was a famous thing from the Pacific Northwest in the ‘80s and ‘90s where environmentalists found a spotted owl, which was allegedly different from other owls and more special than these other owls. And the spotted owl’s habitat was in these woodlands that were being used for logging.

And so, the Endangered Species Act was invoked to restrict logging in these forests, a lot of people lost their jobs, there was a lot of concern from working-class people in Oregon about this. And part of what made the whole thing annoying to me is that the spotted owl — it wasn’t like the unintended consequence of protecting the spotted owl was to shut down the logging industry. The spotted owl was a pretext for shutting down the logging industry.

And there’s a famous case of the snail darter, which is some kind of fish, and this was invoked to prevent a dam from being constructed that was going to provide emission-free electricity to lots and lots of people. And, again — there was a New York Times story about this recently — but there is no such species. They cooked it up in order to have a litigation hook to stop the dam.

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, to be fair, it’s like, “what is a species” is not a thing that is naturally occurring in the world.

Matthew Yglesias: Absolutely. To me, though, this has always been the essence of the environmental movement: is seeking pretexts to do things that are bad for humans in order to preserve nature. And, so, it’s almost like — now, sometimes they hit on something. The spotted owl, I think, is real. The snail darter is fake. The neurological…

Jerusalem Demsas: There is a fish. That’s a real fish.

Matthew Yglesias: Sure, but the…

Jerusalem Demsas: But the thing that happened here is that the Endangered Species Act — if you can count something as being endangered — so, basically, if you’re trying to figure out whether an animal is a new kind of species or just the same fish but it has different stripes — like there are humans that come in very different shapes and sizes, but they’re all humans.

Matthew Yglesias: Sure. But so, like, I could kill a million deer, right?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes…

Matthew Yglesias: And that’s fine, because there’s 10 million deer.

Jerusalem Demsas: It’s not fine.

Matthew Yglesias: No, no, no, but legally speaking. But if it turns out that it’s a special kind of deer, right?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes, where there’s only 200 left.

Matthew Yglesias: The purple-toed deer, right? Then I can’t even kill six of them, because they’re endangered. So there’s this quest, though, for litigation hooks like this snail darter. Because you can stop the dam — because they’re just against dams. And so, again, I feel like…

Jerusalem Demsas: But I think it’s this core thing, right, about “What is the environmental movement actually about?” that is at the heart of what we’re talking about here. So, I think that there are many, many people within the environmental movement — many leaders — who are actually specifically concerned about climate change or wildlife preservation or just natural beauty.

And then there’s hunters and anglers that just want to preserve their wildlife for their sports purposes...

Matthew Yglesias: Can I — I was just in a conversation about hunters and anglers yesterday, and I was pretending to know what an angler is. Does that just mean you fish?

Jerusalem Demsas: I thought so.

Matthew Yglesias: OK.

Jerusalem Demsas: That’s what I thought it was, and now I’m scared.

Matthew Yglesias: Oh, so you’re also faking?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah. “A person who fishes with a rod and line for pleasure.” That’s what I thought it was too.

Matthew Yglesias: Great, OK.

Jerusalem Demsas: I… yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: That’s to distinguish you from the bad fishermen — the commercial trawlers with the nets.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes, with the big nets, yeah.

Something that’s definitely true is that the environmental movement as a large-scale mass movement was really fueled by a lot of suburbanites who were anti-growth. There are a lot of great books about this. Lily Geismer’s Don’t Blame Us is a really great book about this in the Boston context.

But, basically, this idea [was] that there were these people who really cared about the environment, and then they needed to find a mass movement of people. And the people who were attracted to this were often suburbanites who had moved, themselves, in very quote-unquote “anti-environmental ways,” to suburbs of major cities — for the first time in the post-war era, as these suburbs were being literally created — and then were frustrated that there was more development happening, because they wanted that green space.

They wanted their lawns. They didn’t want it to be endless suburbia. And they wanted the last house to be built to be their house, you know? And…

Matthew Yglesias: Well, this happens all the time. People go to Maine because they like nature. And then other people want to come to Maine because they also like nature. But the people who already got there are like, “No, I’m here. I’m here to be near the forest. I cut down trees for my house, but you can’t cut down trees for your house, because I’m trying to be close to the trees.”

Jerusalem Demsas: And this is a core base of the environmental movement. And I don’t think it invalidates the fact that there are — I mean, this becomes politically relevant. Because if your base is not actually people who want to live densely so as to prevent wildlife loss, but actually want to themselves have a house in the suburbs but no one else to, you actually become much more an anti-growth movement than a pro-environment movement.

But, again, I think it’s really important that it doesn’t mean it actually invalidates the existence of these other people. Like, for instance, me and you have been doing this YIMBY thing for a while. You’ve been doing it much longer than I have.

And, if the YIMBY movement ends up getting allies — that we ourselves are like, “Yeah, you just have to have allies in a movement in order to win factional battles in politics” — but then those allies end up being much more powerful than the people who originally cared about housing, and those people are actually, I don’t know, secretly pro-housing but hate immigrants or whatever, that’s not our fault.

Matthew Yglesias: No, no, OK, but that’s not what I’m saying. Like, I feel…

A recurring conversation that I have with Democratic Party members of Congress about permitting-reform negotiations is that they want to get to yes on an energy permitting-reform deal. And every once in a while you meet a member of Congress who has strong views on the merits of this kind of thing. But the vast majority of them don’t.

They want to be for a bill that is an energy permitting-reform bill…

Jerusalem Demsas: To make it easier to permit renewable energy mostly, but also, they’re OK with maybe a little bit of fossil fuels, yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: Understanding that there will be some kind of deal; that it will facilitate renewables and will also do something Republicans like. But they want…

They don’t care what the content of the deal is. They want at least some name-brand environmental groups to say that the deal is good. Because they are not green fanatics who are like, “I’ll only do it if Greenpeace is for it,” but they are not willing to buck the entirety of EDF, NRDC, Sierra, etc. They need a split amongst the groups.

But then, they always tell me, “You know, these groups are way more reasonable than you think, Matt. They’ve cast aside all this, you know, cobwebs of the 1970s,” and so on and so forth. And I just don’t think that that is accurate.

And I don’t mean to say that in a disparaging way. But I think that…

Jerusalem Demsas: What’s the non-disparaging way to say “You still have cobwebs?”

Matthew Yglesias: No, no, no, because what I am trying to do here is actually take seriously the claims of this movement and what its priorities are. And it really is a movement, I think, that is about preserving nature from the encroachments of humanity.

You know, she uses the term — this is outdated — she says “man” a lot, right? But man, or humanity, is an important concept. And this opposition between humanity and nature is an important part of her schema. And the way that it arises most specifically, I think, is not…

This stuff around suburbia has, I think, an important technical aspect to it, and it’s counterintuitive to people that your green lawn is less eco-friendly than my rowhouse block or the apartment building that I grew up in.

But it’s really in the treatment of the Third World, right? That climate change is real. This is real science. It is causing real harms to real human beings.

Most of those harms are occurring in the tropics, both because it’s hotter there already, so there’s a baseline issue, and also because the people who live in the tropics are much poorer, so they have less air conditioning, they’re more likely to be working outside, etc. etc. etc.

Jerusalem Demsas: They can’t build, like…

Matthew Yglesias: Right. But the environmental movement is a million light years away from being a movement whose center value is about benefiting the human beings who live in the tropics. They’re not, like, mixed on housing stuff in the suburbs of Minneapolis, like conflicts, elements of hypocrisy, elements of coalitional dynamic.

But on this core question of economic development in the Third World, I think there isn’t a split. The view is that one of the worst things that has happened is that China and India have become less poor and that that has led to this acceleration of climate risk.

And now they don’t frame it that way, because that makes them sound terrible. But they never, ever, ever say, “Climate change is the downside of a good thing.” It’s instead…

That’s why there’s all this apocalypticism. They want us to believe that it’s net-net worse that China has become richer than Nigeria. And that’s not true, but that’s what they think. Very authentically.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah. I mean, I am, like, half in agreement with what you’re saying, and part of that is, you’re reading this book, right? And this happens all the time with environmentalists where they’ll quote to you these studies about damages. They’re like, “Wow, there’s going to be like $300 million in damages from wildfires. It’s going to be like $1 billion in flood-insurance claims this year, whatever, $3 trillion over the course of the next 10” — whatever. These massive, massive numbers.

And I take that seriously. I’m like, OK, let’s do some cost-benefit analysis about stuff. Because if you are going to engage — and Carson does this too, in her book, where she points out all the economic harms that can accrue to farmers or whoever if you allow this DDT to destroy their crops, or if they become single-crop farmers or whatever, and it’s so clear to me that the point of all of that is then to do cost-benefit analysis about various policy interventions.

Like, “OK, so there are all these costs that come from allowing a coal plant to exist. And then there are all these benefits that will accrue to the local community, because in sub-Saharan Africa, because they will now have energy.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, electricity is good. Air pollution’s bad.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah! Let’s figure out which one of these things is bigger or smaller. And it is also true that the big question then becomes “How are you discounting these things?” Because right now, today, it might be the case that that coal plant is definitely higher benefits than costs, but then what about the millions of people who are going to live in the future?

And this is a big live debate under the Biden administration. They tried to make the social cost of greenhouse gases larger, to change the formula such that it cared more about future people. And even ourselves in the future — so, us two years from now, I should care about that person somewhat more than we do in our preexisting accounting.

But you’re correct that the modern environmental movement is not a massive cost-benefit analysis movement. But I think that largely the reason for this is that they’re a very local movement.

Like, climate change is a global problem, and you have to care about this “How much someone emits in Topeka, Kansas, is not really relevant if 20 more people therefore are emitting in Illinois,” you know what I mean? So you have to care about these things at a global scale.

But the environmental movement itself, because of the nature of — you’re observing things happening around you and you’re like, “That thing is bad, I want to stop it, and the cause of that is this other broader thing” — means that they don’t really mostly pay attention to what’s happening outside of the country at all, except very small groups.

And so, I think what ends up happening is: I don’t think there’s actually a viewpoint of most environmental groups in the United States about development in China. There’s a small number of environmentalists who are involved in that sort of thing through the World Bank, where they’re like, “Well, we don’t want to finance any fossil-fuel industries.”

And I just think that it’s a little bit unfair to say these knock-on effects of this ideology have produced something really bad — which I agree with are bad — and then blame it on people who are like, “Hey, I just like to see snail darters.”

Matthew Yglesias: So, I’ve heard this localism-globalism kind of characterization of the situation. I think David Roberts, who I used to work with at Vox, has often framed it this way, in trying to push environmentalism out of — he’s like a big green YIMBY, in part for this reason.

I have doubts, though, because one thing that I fight about all the time with people these days is natural gas. And part of the deal with natural gas is that it is somewhat lower emissions, from a greenhouse gas standpoint, than coal, on a “per-magawatt basis.”

Jerusalem Demsas: “MAGAwatt?”

Matthew Yglesias: Megawatt. But on a particulate-air-pollution basis, it’s dramatically cleaner. So, in a theoretical world where you have people who are just very, very concerned about localist environmental issues, you would just be really, really enthusiastic about natural gas:

It’s displacing coal plants. It’s doing an incredible amount to clean up localized air pollution. And I could see a world where the environmental movement was so gung-ho about natural gas that I’m like, “Hey, guys, you gotta think about the impact this is having on Bangladesh.”

But that’s not at all their viewpoint about natural gas. I feel like what actually goes on is a kind of argument in the alternative, where when somebody says, “Well, we should build a utility-scale solar power plant,” then the local environmental harm of cutting down the trees is very, very salient.

But if somebody says, “Well, we should build natural-gas pipelines to drive coal off the grid,” then it’s like, “Oh no, guys, we gotta think about the climate change.” And it’s just — because what’s actually happening is that you have a movement that does not value the energy at all.

Jerusalem Demsas: But I think that what you’re describing — and, again, the thing that’s interesting here is that you and I are largely aligned about the environmental movement being oppositional to economic progress. But I think the thing that you’re describing here is largely that movements create heuristics. They’re often not doing this — it’s very difficult to imagine a movement being very, very good at delineating between specific policy areas in a very granular way.

Instead, they come up with things like, “OK, in general, coal plants are bad. In general, fossil fuel is really, really bad.” And, obviously, the Industrial Revolution happened and created modernity and the possibility for billions of people to live on this planet and not die of being too hot in the summer, but, at the same time, the fact that the environmental movement has these overly simplistic heuristics around fossil fuels: it’s still largely correct.

You should be broadly for less carbon-intensive forms of energy. That is just broadly true. Now, in the specifics, the fact that, then, every single thing that could potentially use carbon emissions or byproducts is then vilified to the point of ridiculousness is bad. But I think that if we’re talking about the specific core of what environmentalism is…

Movements themselves, especially ones that are this large, are just going to have these disparate elements in them. And I agree that the base of the American environmental movement in particular is largely kind of an anti-growth suburban who just likes green stuff around them, and is therefore unconcerned about the large-scale impacts on Bangladesh or whatever or China or what’s going on in Nigeria.

Like, yeah, that’s true. That’s true of all American politics. There’s not, there’s just no care about, like…

Matthew Yglesias: Yes, yes, yes, but this is why I keep bringing up the fact that, but then they do invoke climate change as this trump card, as if they do care.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think these are different people.

Matthew Yglesias: I don’t think that — well, OK. I guess I am going to give Rachel Carson her due. Which is to say that, even though I believe — I don’t really think that people who I disagree with are being untrue to the values of this book, because she does invoke these capital-R Romantic conversations — there is also, within this text, the seeds of, I think, a much better set of ideas.

Her discussion of the screwworm is genuinely visionary. She’s saying this is a good idea, and is a little skeptical that we will ever bring it to the adequate scale — and we did. And we ought to bring it to even bigger scale. And she was right to champion this early on. As well as being…

I mean, she was just literally correct about insecticide resistance to DDT. She was right that neurotoxins damage mammals and birds as well as insects. This is all literally true. And the aspect of this book that is about finding better ways to address the insect problem…

I mean, it both has helped people with insect and human health, but I also think it’s a very good way to think about these questions: that if we have a concern that is valid, but there’s also downsides to the human activity, we should try to think of a better way to do it, right?

And that’s eco-modernism, in a way. And they could try to claim her as a progenitor there too...

Jerusalem Demsas: So, one thing I did want to talk about, though, is the — we’re in a book club now, Matt. This is our second book. Our first book, if folks didn’t hear it, was Betty Friedan’s The Feminine Mystique, which is largely a book written — What year did that come out?

Matthew Yglesias: Same, ‘62, right? Same time.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, yeah. Mystique, ‘63.

Jerusalem Demsas: So it comes out immediately after this. The Feminine Mystique is a book about how women basically need to go back into the workforce. They need to go get degrees and jobs, and they need to return to the values and life trajectories of the women from the 1930s. And what’s interesting is that that book is literally just talking about Rachel Carson.

Rachel Carson, in the 1920s, goes to college, she gets a master’s degree, she wants to get a PhD, but the Great Depression happens, and she has to make money for her family. So she ends up not doing that.

But she’s a lifelong worker and public intellectual. She’s writing in the New Yorker and various other places…

Matthew Yglesias: It’s also cool she’s working for the… Fisheries Service?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, she’s at the Department of Interior.

Matthew Yglesias: But also just doing takes?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes. Yeah, exactly.

Matthew Yglesias: It’s great. We used to have a country.

Jerusalem Demsas: We did. And it’s incredible, because it’s not just about her life that Betty Friedan is literally saying, like, “Women in the post-war: You should be more like Rachel Carson.”

But the reaction to her is so gendered. It’s kind of wild. It’s the kind of thing that you would not really expect now.

But, very much, she’s talked about as this kind of crazy woman who — I mean, she doesn’t have kids; she doesn’t get married. There’s some suspicion that she was maybe a lesbian, or she’s attracted to women. She had some long-term, potentially romantic relationship with this woman she lived near for some time.

Matthew Yglesias: It’s a “Boston marriage,” as we used to say.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah. Um, I did not know that term, but…

Matthew Yglesias: Google it.

Jerusalem Demsas: I will, I’ll Claude later.

But the reaction to her from the chemical industry is very much, “This woman does not have a PhD, she has no standing to be having this conversation, she does this lady science, obviously, biology.”

And at the same time, too, there’s all these things about how maybe she’s a communist sympathizer, and she hates modernity, and they try to commission this book about how everyone in the world would die and be overrun by insects if she had her way.

And what’s so interesting and what this really made me think of is: Both you and I have written about this progressive omnicause, right? This idea that if you are in favor of one progressive viewpoint around environmentalism, you also have to be in favor of really radical viewpoints or progressive viewpoints on race and gender and economic populism, or whatever it is. And all these kinds of groups sort of hold the line.

And in many ways, I think we underrate how much this was created by conservative backlash. What you are witnessing here, in the reaction to her book, is they are actually stitching together all of these things for her.

Out of the reaction to her, the feminists really come out in her defense, and feminist environmentalism becomes much more of a thing — this idea that, A) breast cancer, which was largely understudied in many ways. There’s a lot of critiques that it was because scientists cared less about this illness that affected women.

And the cancer being linked to DDT really created this sense of, “Oh, this male establishment of scientists hates women, they hate the environment.” She herself is obviously talking, in a very concerned way, about the way that men dominate, quote-unquote, “the environment” — “man,” she says — but she’s also gendering it, too, herself.

And it made me really think about how much the reaction to specific progressive viewpoints really created this necessity for this coalition to develop. Because they’re calling her a communist for saying DDT has some problems and we should look into them. And she is cast as, “OK, you must be a communist, you’re a feminist, you didn’t have children, you’re a spinster lady” — I mean, literally, they are projecting all this onto her.

Some of it might be true, I don’t know, not really clear. I don’t think she was a communist. I think she was actually notably not a feminist or something. It was the 1930s, ‘60s, whatever it was.

Matthew Yglesias: It’s funny, actually. So, it comes from — it was the former Agriculture Secretary, Ezra Benson, writes a letter to Eisenhower in which he says that she’s unmarried, but she was pretty. And so he says, “Well, she’s probably a communist.”

Matthew Yglesias: This is a more innocent time. And I think the modern suspicion that she’s probably a lesbian makes a little bit more sense of the biographical facts. I mean, yes, that is all true. You see something of that.

Jerusalem Demsas: I also think, like, don’t take the bait, right? You know what I mean? The thing that makes her so successful is that, actually, she was so specifically focused on this thing that was obviously true and was very persuasive about it.

And she wasn’t super bombastic. She got on CBS — we’ll play a clip of it or whatever — but she’s very muted and just discussing the facts, etc. She doesn’t start talking about the racialized element or whatever, you know?

Matthew Yglesias: No, so part of the breakdown that happens in the late ‘60s is the institutional landscape is very different at the time that she’s writing. And so you have: The whole government is just organized in some different ways back then. And it would have been possible, in theory, for the institutions that existed circa 1962 to say, “You know what? A lot of information has come out over the past decade about the harms of insecticide spraying, and so we are going to pull back. We are going to…”

Because you used to have these agriculture departments essentially supervising what you were allowed to do, farm-wise. And they could have incorporated this critique into their practice, and they largely didn’t.

As you’re saying, there’s this kind of massive resistance coming from the industry, but also from the institutions that she was saying had been lax. And so what happens, as the Clean Air Act and the Clean Water Act are coming together, is that a different institutional setup is created.

Instead of just saying the existing government departments that were supposed to be supervising this are going to do a better job now, we create these new departments that have this new mandate to kind of swoop in — burst in, like Kool-Aid Man style — and say, “You can’t do that.”

And you also have mechanisms — there’s a very brief discussion of this in the book, but I think it winds up being really significant, where she’s like, “And who decided that we should be allowed to do this?” And she phrases it as a rhetorical question, rather than literally saying, “This one guy decided, and that was a bad decision, and you should defeat him at the next election, and then a different guy will make a different decision.”

She puts forward the idea that letting the elected officials and their appointees make these decisions is, like, per se bad, and that there should be some kind of community veto power and joint governance over this kind of thing. And that’s not an idea that she elaborates on at great length, or really thinks a lot about, but really was taken over.

The first-order legacy of this book is more restriction on chemical spraying, but the higher-order restriction is a greatly expanded litigation frontier, right? Just much more willingness to say, “I don’t like the way the government officials came down on this, so I’m going to go to court.” And I think that has been quite problematic for America, that we’ve become this vetocracy in which everything…

And this is something environmentalists are often annoyed with: If you want to put bike lanes on the street, now people can come sue you, and they’ll say, “Oh, you didn’t do enough Clean Air Act studies about whether bicycles are cleaner than cars.” And you’ll show up and you’ll be like, “What the fuck are you talking about? It’s a bicycle.” But they’ll be like, “But if you make the road thinner, the cars might idle more, so that might be more…” You know?

And it’s like, you could study these things to death, and nobody is empowered to just be like, “You know what? I’ve read the research, I listened to people, I made my decision. If you don’t like it, beat me at the fucking election.” And everyone’s mad about this now, but it is…

Jerusalem Demsas: No, I mean, I do think this is the place where I’m the most opposed to the parts of the book where she — I couldn’t find the specific quotes in there about where she says something like, “Who decided for all of us that this happened?” Or she says, “We were never consulted.”

And it’s much more like a — there’s even an anecdote about an individual homeowner who was never notified or whatever. And there are certain things which I think are true. I think if you’re going to do mass spraying of something that could harm dogs, you should tell everyone you’re going to do that thing, keep their dogs inside for the day, whatever. These things are true that come out of this period, about public notification, processes or whatever.

But you and I have been railing against public comment for so long, because people hear that and they’re like, “Oh yeah, of course the people should get a chance to comment and be involved in the policy proceedings.” And as a principle, that’s true, but then the question is how you operationalize that.

Because does that mean that, at any point when a congressperson is trying to draft legislation, that they’re not allowed to do that without inviting public comment into every single part of that process? It would literally make it impossible to do anything if we took that to the most extreme of the logic here, right?

Matthew Yglesias: Well, also, I sincerely don’t want to weigh in — I don’t want to research which kind of insecticide treatments are appropriate…

Jerusalem Demsas: Totally.

Matthew Yglesias: …and develop an informed opinion and weigh in.

Jerusalem Demsas: And you don’t have the expertise!

Matthew Yglesias: It’s like, I agree. It seemed like they were making a bad call before. But I would like elected officials to have a bureaucracy that tries to evaluate this evidence and make a good decision. I don’t want me and my friends and my random neighbors to just all be…

Jerusalem Demsas: Consulted.

Matthew Yglesias: …weighing in on this.

Jerusalem Demsas: But I think, to me, too, this is actually a really important point, because I think there are a lot of people who are in kind of the YIMBY-abundance camp who have really conceptualized the environmental movement as, “Oh, you know, it was a reaction to the big government era, and now we’re having a necessary counter-reaction.”

I’m like, no. We could have reacted to all of this appropriately by saying, “OK, we need to, A.) Update the science. Elected officials need to put more pressure on agencies to pressure-test the claims by these chemists and to make sure that, if they are okaying certain kinds of chemicals to be used for normal spraying purposes, that those are not hurting the food supply or giving people cancer.

And you could do that without restructuring all of democracy such that random individual people or groups can block our ability to do — whether it’s bike lanes, whether it’s apartment buildings, whether it’s transmission lines, whether it’s solar panels that are happening.

Like, I have literally visited sites of proposed solar panels where I’m being told by environmentalist groups that, “Oh, we’re concerned about the knock-on effects on other people of building these solar panels.” I go to this place. It’s the middle of fucking — like, there’s no way it’s affecting…

Matthew Yglesias: But, also, to give the environmentalists their due, the other thing that will happen is: Like, you’ve seen this in New England — oil companies will use an environmental impact litigation to say you can’t build this transmission line from Canada. Because you can’t write a law that says, “Come on, you’re an oil company. Like, this is obviously bad faith.”

Jerusalem Demsas: They’re part of the public.

Matthew Yglesias: Right. Well, it’s a system of laws, so anybody who wants to — the evil chemical industry...

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, there was one where a literal competitor — they’re both renewable energy companies — and a literal competitor was using the public input process to destroy their competitor’s ability to site their new energy project. It’s just like…

And, obviously, if your claim is that we need a public process by which there’s no way for an agency to go, “You’re being ridiculous. As a discretionary matter, I’ve heard you out. I don’t agree. If you think I’ve done something horrible, gin up an election and someone will fire me.” You could do that, but instead we’ve created a system which…

And again, part of what’s going on in this time period is that you have way more college-educated people are lay-people. There’s more college people in general. And they’re looking around and they’re like, “Oh, the person authorizing all of this is not more educated than me. I’m a lawyer, I’m a scientist, I’m a doctor. I can go, now, read the documents that are created or the scientific studies.”

I was just talking to someone in Montgomery County, Maryland, who was incensed by the boundary reanalysis that they’re doing over there. And she herself is an economist, so she’s looking at their studies, and she’s just like, “This is so badly done, this is terribly done,” whatever.

And before, it was sort of like, “Well, I can’t really evaluate what the government’s doing.” Now I can. You find out that maybe you’re smarter than them. Maybe they have made some mistakes. And that reduces your faith in the governmental process.

Which I think is a rational and reasonable way to react to it. But then, what happens is, OK, well, sometimes your objection is not actually, “I think that you have not accurately assessed the benefits and costs and come to a conclusion that is defensible.” It is, “I don’t want an apartment building near me, so I’m going to use my acumen and my legal training to gum up this process forever.”

And I think it becomes very, very difficult, because right now, when you’re reading this book, and I think most people are sort of just like, “Yeah, of course she was just totally correct,” and that ends up validating this entire new system of participatory democracy that actually made it difficult to become more environmentally conscious.

Matthew Yglesias: There’s a faux-technical aspect to it. So, when school boundaries get redrawn in the District of Columbia, it’s done sort of on the basis of technical considerations. The official document will give some technical reasons about population growth and commuting logistics and blah blah blah.

But there’s just certain schools that people want their kids to be assigned to and other schools that they don’t want them to be assigned to. And if they don’t get what they want, they complain.

And I always feel like it would be better to sort of admit that that’s what is happening and that the elected officials literally cannot make everybody happy, because not everybody can be assigned to the one middle school that everybody wants to be assigned to.

And so, they are making a decision, and they are in fact responsible for making the decision — that there’s this bizarre school-boundary gerrymandering to send people who live in Mount Pleasant to this one particular middle school west of Rock Creek Park, and it’s just done that way because those people got access to that school a long time ago, when the population was different, and they don’t want to give it up, and the city council is deferring to them out of status quo bias. And there is no reason.

But, instead, you have to go through a lot of BS around some of this stuff, because sometimes you just face trade-offs, like between the birds and the farmers. And sometimes there’s a technical problem and the thing the farmers are trying to do won’t work. But sometimes what the farmers want is just bad for the birds. And what the Audubon Society wants is just bad for farmers. And that’s why we have democracy.

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, there’s a lot of trade-off denialism, I think. This is part of what I think — when I was really involved in environmental activism, before I became a journalist — there’s a lot of messaging to make it seem like there’s actually no harm being accrued to anyone else.

So, this idea that, “Oh, renewables are cheaper,” which on very specific metrics is true. The cost of solar panels now is really low. But when you take a look at the totality, like, OK, if you just killed all fossil fuels and…

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, making fossil fuels illegal is not cheaper than allowing them to continue to exist.

Jerusalem Demsas: Exactly. But, again, the way that some of these things are used is, “OK, there’s no trade-offs. It can be cheaper, it’ll be better for labor because there are all these jobs that’ll be created.”

And there are a lot of jobs created by electrification. A lot of those jobs are not jobs that are easily unionized in the same way these coal plants or whatever could be. And they’re also often worse in some ways — they’re more transient. If you’re installing rooftop solar, it’s not a job where you go to the same place every day. You’re going to different places, and eventually you tap out the market, and then… You know what I mean?

Matthew Yglesias: Sure. There’s only so many roofs.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, and they’re also not really going to be high-paying in the same way that you would want. But there’s all these messages like, “Oh, you know, definitely you could just have high-paying, great green jobs. There’s no tension between labor and environmental.” There’s even this thing called the Blue-Green Alliance, which is an attempt to really forge these connections across.

And I think all of that stuff is good, and I think it’s good to have a positive vision. But it can get to the point where you are just doing trade-off denialism, and you can’t actually just make tough calls and admit, “Oh, you’re losing, but I think this is more important.” Then we can have an actual debate over whether that’s true.

Matthew Yglesias: Speaking of trade-offs…

Jerusalem Demsas: Time to talk about papers?

Matthew Yglesias: Peer review?

Jerusalem Demsas: Peer review!

Matthew Yglesias: Peer review.

Jerusalem Demsas: OK, peer review.

Matthew Yglesias: What’s cooking?

Jerusalem Demsas: This is the segment of the podcast where Matt and I talk about a new white paper that’s out there in the world. It’s my turn this time.

OK, so, this is a paper called “Does Employment Slow Cognitive Decline? Evidence from Labor Market Shocks“

Matthew Yglesias: Does it?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes, it does. It does.

Matthew Yglesias: I hate these question titles in the papers.

Jerusalem Demsas: I love question title papers!

Yeah, anyway, the authors are Noah Arman Kouchekinia, David Neumark, and Tim Bruckner. This just came out in NBER last month.

The large finding here is that when older people leave the workforce, they experience cognitive decline. The effect is concentrated among men 51 to 64. And one of the things that’s really difficult with stuff like this is people are like, “OK, is it the fact that people who leave the labor force earlier are just already experiencing cognitive decline, or is it that staying in the labor force actually makes you more cognitively sharp or whatever?”

And they overcome this by looking at, they look at commuting zones, which are the areas that people will commute in. And…

Matthew Yglesias: It’s a zone.

Jerusalem Demsas: It’s a zone. Yeah. So, basically, they’re looking at average effects across entire communities so that the individual things that are going on with a person can wash out. But I think this is really relevant, this whole paper, in... I think people know…

Matthew Yglesias: So, the research design is that sometimes your whole area suffers a negative economic shock. And older people are just more likely to take early retirement when the local labor market deteriorates.

Jerusalem Demsas: So they look at places where there was that negative shock and compare those to places where there wasn’t. And then, similarly, old people — they can look at both of those areas and just go, “Well, what happened?”

Matthew Yglesias: So, instead of comparing a real-estate broker who retires early to his coworker in the same office who didn’t — because then you get the endogeneity problem — they’re just saying, well, if there’s a downturn and, in general, more people are retiring early, what happens to the cognitive acuity of people who’ve suffered this geographical labor-market shock?

Jerusalem Demsas: And it’s not good.

I mean, this, to me, is relevant in this moment right now, where we’re all talking about what happens if there’s no more work in the age of Artificial General Intelligence. And I think most people have experienced this in their own life, like, “Oh, retirement early, it’s not good for old people in your lives. Sometimes they don’t have things to do, etc.”

But at the same time, it’s also the thing people are aiming for. There’s this whole community online called FIRE, where they’re all trying to retire super early. Like, “How do I save enough money and put it in index funds so I can retire at like 35, or 40, or 50 or whatever?”

And I’m of two minds about this. Work as “desirable for purpose and for giving your brain something to do,” is good and true, but, at the same time, I do think we get to a very big “make-work” kind of attitude about things when we take it to its logical extension.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, so there’s a couple of debates that this is relevant to. One is this kind of AI labor-market displacement. The other is just straightforward entitlement-type stuff.

Because a perennial tension in American politics is that we are spending more and more money on Social Security and Medicare. The population’s become older.

One of the most fiscally straightforward ways to address this is to raise the retirement age and get people to work longer. This is politically challenging; it’s one of the most unpopular things you can do, because people have positive feelings about the elderly.

So, if you can marshal evidence that it’s actually low-key bad for people to be retiring earlier, that would unwind this political knot, right, and help budget wonks solve a big problem that they have. And so I think that’s one reason that…

I’ve heard about this literature from a lot of people who are professional deficit hawks. A question about this is: This is a clever research design, but are you now researching the wrong thing?

You talked about the early-retirement people, and I think it’s possible that there’s a systematic difference between someone who has deliberately structured their whole life around the idea of amassing savings, being frugal, planning for early retirement — and has presumably come up with something they want to do with their early retirement — and somebody who is bopping along and is like, “I’ll try to work till I’m 67, when I can collect the full value of my Social Security benefits,” but then you lose your job at 60, and you’re like, “You know what, I’m not gonna master a new career in my mid-60s. I’m just gonna put in for early retirement and sort of peace out.”

And you can see why that could put you on a bad trajectory in life — because, precisely because you’re identifying people based on an external shock, it’s not what they wanted to do.

If people had been saying, “Hey, Matt, we love your wisdom as an old-man pundit, please come on my podcast, tell the kids what they’re doing wrong,” that would be awesome. Whereas, if it’s like, “I have no subscribers, nobody gives a shit about me, and I’m like, “Well, I guess I’m retired now,” that’s kind of a bummer.
